title: Rosemary focaccia
subtitle: Thick, pillowy Italian flatbread with rosemary and flaky salt
excerpt: A thick, oily Italian flatbread with a soft, open crumb and a crisp base. The dough is made wetter than a standard bread dough and proved in the tin, then dimpled all over with oiled fingers before baking to create the characteristic pockets that hold olive oil. Fresh rosemary and flaky sea salt go on top. Focaccia is forgiving and well-suited to first-time bread bakers.
sourceNotes: Focaccia is documented in Ligurian and Genoese food literature from at least the sixteenth century as a flatbread baked in olive oil. Pellegrino Artusi, La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene (1891, freely available at Project Gutenberg IT), includes an olive oil flatbread consistent with the focaccia tradition. The modern Ligurian focaccia al rosmarino is the most widely known regional style and is described in Italian baking literature throughout the twentieth century.

content.text: firmly with oiled fingers just before it goes into the oven. Then pour over a generous amount of olive oil so the dimples fill. The oil steams under the dough during baking and gives focaccia its soft, almost fried base.

attrs.prepNote: plus extra for the tin and topping

content.text: Combine the flour, yeast, and salt in a large bowl. Pour in the warm water and olive oil and mix to a rough dough. Turn out and knead for 8 to 10 minutes until smooth and slightly tacky. Alternatively, mix and knead in a stand mixer on medium speed for 6 minutes. Place in a lightly oiled bowl, cover, and leave to rise in a warm place for 1 hour until doubled.

Pour 3 tablespoons of olive oil into a 30 x 40 cm rimmed tin. Tip the dough into the tin and press it gently toward the edges. If it springs back, leave it uncovered for 10 minutes to relax, then press again. Cover loosely and prove for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 220°C conventional (200°C fan).

Drizzle 2 tablespoons of olive oil over the proved dough. With well-oiled fingertips, press straight down firmly all over the surface to create deep dimples. Scatter the rosemary leaves into the dimples and sprinkle over the flaky salt. Bake for 22 to 26 minutes until deep golden on top, with a crisp, golden base. Transfer to a rack and cool for 10 minutes before cutting.
